# Checklist — Software Security (SWEBOK v4, Chapter 13)

> Reference checklist derived from **SWEBOK Guide v4.0, Chapter 13 — Software Security** (pp. 13-1 → 13-8), with cross-references to ISO/IEC 27001, ISO/IEC 27000, ISO/IEC 15408 (Common Criteria) and CVE/CWE/CAPEC/CVSS.
> Each row defines one verification check that should be answered **at requirements time** so the SRS encodes verifiable security requirements, not only generic claims.
> **Ordered by severity of a *missing* check** (Critical → High → Medium → Low). See severity scale in [checklist-software-requirements.md](checklist-software-requirements.md).

## Checklist

| ID | Check name | Reference (§ / page) | Severity | What must be verified | Concrete example (✅ success / ❌ failure) |
|----|------------|----------------------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| SS-001 | CIA properties explicitly defined per data class | §1.2 Information Security `[13-1]` | Critical | For every data class processed, the spec states Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability requirements (and, when relevant, Authenticity, Accountability, Non-repudiation, Reliability). | ✅ Data classification table: PII (C: high, I: high, A: medium), system logs (C: medium, I: high, A: high) — each with concrete controls. ❌ Spec lists features but never says which data must be protected or how. |
| SS-002 | Security requirements elicited (not only assumed) | §4.1 Security Requirements `[13-3, 13-4]` | Critical | Security requirements go through elicitation, specification and prioritization. Misuse / abuse cases, threat actors and risk assessment outputs are part of the requirements set. | ✅ SRS §6.3 cites misuse cases MU-01..MU-12 from a STRIDE-based threat model; risks rated by likelihood × impact; top 5 risks have mitigation REQs. ❌ Security paragraph says "use HTTPS"; no threat model, no abuse cases. |
| SS-003 | Threat model produced and reviewed | §4.2 Security Design `[13-4]` (threat modeling) | Critical | A threat model (STRIDE, PASTA, attack trees, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, …) is produced, reviewed by security + dev + ops, and re-evaluated at major changes. | ✅ STRIDE per data flow diagram; reviewed each major release; mitigations traced to REQs. ❌ "We thought about security" — no artifact, no review. |
| SS-004 | Compliance obligations enumerated and bound to requirements | §2.2 ISMS / ISO 27001 `[13-2, 13-3]`; §4.1 derivation | Critical | All applicable laws/regulations/standards (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, NIS2, DORA, ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A, SOC 2, ISO 27017/27018, FedRAMP, sector-specific) are listed and each obligation traces to one or more REQs. | ✅ Compliance matrix maps "GDPR Art. 32 — security of processing → REQ-SEC-014..028"; ISMS roles named. ❌ Compliance assumed implicit; the very first GDPR Art. 15 access request takes 6 weeks. |
| SS-005 | Access control model defined (authentication, authorisation, accountability) | §4.2 Security Design `[13-4]`; CERT #6 Least Privilege `[13-5]` | Critical | The spec defines the authentication mechanism, the authorisation model (RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC), session management, MFA scope, federation/SSO, service-to-service auth, and accountability/audit logging. | ✅ RBAC matrix of roles × permissions; MFA mandatory for admin; service identities use mTLS; audit log immutable. ❌ "Admin can do everything"; auth/authz inferred from screen mockups. |
| SS-006 | Input validation requirement explicit (and applied at trust boundary) | CERT #1 Validate input `[13-5]`; §4.4 Construction `[13-4]` | Critical | Every trust boundary (HTTP, message bus, file upload, IPC, RPC, CLI) has explicit input validation requirements (schema, length, range, charset, format, business rules). | ✅ All public endpoints validate against an OpenAPI schema + business invariants; CWE-20 mitigated. ❌ Backend assumes "the UI already validated" — easy bypass via direct API call. |
| SS-007 | Cryptography requirements specified (algorithms, key management, transit, rest) | §4.2 Security Design — cryptography `[13-4]` | Critical | Approved algorithms, modes, key sizes; key generation, distribution, rotation, revocation, storage (HSM/KMS); TLS version & cipher suites; encryption at rest scope. | ✅ "TLS 1.3 only; AES-256-GCM at rest in KMS; keys rotated every 90 days; FIPS 140-3 module on prod." ❌ "Encrypted" with no algorithm/mode/key management; ends up using MD5 + ECB. |
| SS-008 | Secure SDLC adopted; security gates in CI/CD | §3.1 SDLC / DevSecOps `[13-3]` | Critical | The development life cycle includes mandatory security activities at each phase (requirements review, threat model, secure design review, SAST/DAST/SCA in CI, penetration test before release, security smoke tests in prod). | ✅ Pipeline blocks PR on SAST high, SCA CVSS ≥ 7, secrets detection, IaC scan; pre-release pen-test artifact required. ❌ Security is a "phase at the end"; release schedule eats the security review. |
| SS-009 | Default-deny / least privilege baked in | CERT #5 Default deny, #6 Least privilege `[13-5]` | Critical | The spec mandates default-deny on access decisions and least-privilege for users, services and infrastructure components (incl. cloud IAM, DB grants, network policies). | ✅ "All resources default-deny; explicit grants tracked in IaC; quarterly access review." ❌ Service accounts have `*:*` permissions; first compromised service yields total takeover. |
| SS-010 | Third-party / supply-chain risk addressed | §1 Introduction (third-party) `[13-1]`; §4.6 Vulnerability Management `[13-5]` | Critical | OSS/COTS components are inventoried (SBOM), scanned (SCA against CVE/CWE), licence-checked; transitive dependencies are pinned; provenance/signature checks (e.g., SLSA, Sigstore) where applicable. | ✅ CycloneDX SBOM generated per build; SCA blocks high CVEs; container base image is signed and rebuilt weekly. ❌ `npm install` with no lockfile; first supply-chain attack (e.g., typosquat) lands in prod. |
| SS-011 | Vulnerability management process defined (CVE/CWE/CAPEC/CVSS) | §4.6 Vulnerability Management `[13-5]` | Critical | The org has a documented vulnerability disclosure and management process: ingestion of CVE feeds, triage with CVSS, SLA per severity, customer notification path. | ✅ "Critical (CVSS ≥ 9) patched ≤ 7 days; High ≤ 30; security advisories published; security.txt + PGP key online." ❌ CVEs found by a customer; no responsible-disclosure email; long social-media incident. |
| SS-012 | Secure logging & audit trail requirements stated | §1.2 Accountability `[13-1]`; CERT #7 Sanitize data sent `[13-5]` | High | Security-relevant events (authn, authz, admin actions, secret access, config change) are logged with actor, timestamp, before/after, correlation ID; logs are tamper-evident, retained per policy, with PII minimisation. | ✅ Append-only audit log signed daily; retained 1 year hot + 7 years cold; PII masked; SIEM ingestion. ❌ Application log only "user logged in"; no actor for admin actions; logs deleted after 7 days. |
| SS-013 | Secrets management requirement specified | §4.4 Construction `[13-4]` | High | The spec forbids secrets in source / config files / images; specifies the secret store (Vault / KMS / cloud-native), rotation, revocation and access policy. | ✅ "All secrets in HashiCorp Vault; auto-rotation every 30d; no secrets in env files committed to repo; CI scans for secrets." ❌ AWS keys in `.env.production` committed to Git. |
| SS-014 | Defense in depth principle declared | CERT #8 Defense in depth `[13-5]` | High | Multiple independent layers of control protect each asset (network, identity, application, data, monitoring). No single control failure leads to compromise. | ✅ Public endpoint = WAF + auth proxy + app-level authz + DB row-level security + audit; bypass of one still blocks. ❌ A single network ACL is "the" control; a misconfig opens everything. |
| SS-015 | Keep-it-simple / minimise attack surface | CERT #4 Keep it simple `[13-5]` | High | The design avoids unnecessary complexity, unused endpoints/protocols, debug interfaces in production; minimises the attack surface. | ✅ Production image has no shell, no debug agent, no unused ports; feature flags disable unused modules. ❌ Debug endpoint shipped to prod returns DB schema dumps. |
| SS-016 | Misuse & abuse cases inform negative requirements | §4.1 Security Requirements `[13-3, 13-4]`; §4.5 Security Testing `[13-5]` | High | The SRS contains explicit *negative* requirements (what the system shall **not** allow) derived from misuse/abuse cases, mapped to security tests. | ✅ "The system shall NOT allow a non-admin user to view orders of other users (MU-04 → TC-SEC-018)." ❌ Only positive features specified; first authz bypass is found post-release. |
| SS-017 | Security testing scope agreed (static + dynamic + pen-test) | §4.5 Security Testing `[13-5]`; §5.2 Pen-Test Tools `[13-6]` | High | The plan specifies SAST scope, DAST scope, fuzzing of high-risk inputs, and an authorised penetration test with explicit rules of engagement before release. | ✅ Per release: SAST high = 0, DAST high = 0, fuzzing of parser/protocol surfaces 4 h, third-party pen-test with signed SOW. ❌ "Security tests" = a single OWASP ZAP scan with default config. |
| SS-018 | Failure & recovery are secure (fail securely) | §4.2 Security Design — failing and recovering securely `[13-4]` | High | The spec requires that error paths, retries and recovery do not weaken security (e.g., do not fall back to plaintext, do not leak secrets in stack traces, do not bypass auth). | ✅ "On TLS handshake failure: drop connection; never fall back to HTTP. On crash: redact secrets in logs; no autologin after recovery." ❌ A captive-portal-style fallback opens HTTP on TLS failure. |
| SS-019 | Assumptions are validated or documented | §4.4 Construction `[13-4]` | High | All security-relevant assumptions (about callers, environment, trust boundaries, configuration) are explicitly validated at runtime, or — when not possible — documented for installers and maintainers. | ✅ "Assumes ingress runs behind WAF X; if absent, app refuses to start and emits CRITICAL log." ❌ Code assumes "request always comes through gateway"; direct calls bypass authn. |
| SS-020 | Privacy by design / data minimisation requirement | §6 / Privacy testing (cf. Testing KA §2.2.9) | High | Personal data collected, processed and stored is minimised; legal basis identified; data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability) have user-facing flows and acceptance criteria. | ✅ "Collect only email + hashed pwd; no phone unless 2FA; subject-access workflow returns export ≤ 30 days." ❌ Spec asks for "all the data we might need" + 5-year retention with no basis. |
| SS-021 | Data retention & disposal rules defined | §2.2 ISMS `[13-2]`; cf. SR template §4.4 | High | Retention period and disposal method (incl. backups, replicas, caches, logs, residual data, archives) per data class. Conforming to the strictest applicable regulation. | ✅ "Customer PII: retain 24 months active + 12 months cold; cryptographically erased on expiry; backups expire same window." ❌ "Keep forever just in case"; GDPR audit finding. |
| SS-022 | Domain-specific security addressed (cloud, IoT, ML) | §6 Domain-Specific Software Security `[13-6]` | Medium | If the system uses containers/cloud: forgotten-asset / misconfiguration controls. If IoT: endpoint hardening, secure device-to-device, device credibility. If ML: defenses against poisoning & evasion. | ✅ "Cloud: CSPM scan + budget alarms + tag policy; IoT: secure boot + mutual auth + OTA signing; ML: input integrity check + adversarial test suite." ❌ Cloud bills explode on forgotten VMs; ML model fooled by trivial adversarial input. |
| SS-023 | Common Criteria / formal evaluation considered when procurement-driven | §3.2 Common Criteria `[13-3]` | Medium | When customers/regulators require, Common Criteria (ISO/IEC 15408) Protection Profile / Security Target conformance is included in the requirements; assurance level (EAL) chosen. | ✅ "EAL4+ conformance per PP X; Security Target document attached as SRS appendix." ❌ Government tender lost because CC conformance was never planned. |
| SS-024 | Security skills / responsibilities assigned | §2 Security Management `[13-2]`; §2.3 Agile `[13-3]` | Medium | Security roles are named (security champion, threat-model owner, incident commander on call); training plan and security-team involvement in iterations are scheduled. | ✅ "Each squad has a security champion; weekly threat-model office hours; quarterly secure-coding training." ❌ "Security is everyone's job" — and so nobody's. |
| SS-025 | Tool support (SAST / SCA / DAST / IAST / secrets / IaC) selected | §5.1 Vulnerability Checking Tools `[13-5, 13-6]` | Medium | The toolchain is chosen, owned, and integrated into CI/CD with thresholds; tools and their version baselines are listed in the SRS or constitution. | ✅ "SAST: SonarQube; SCA: OSV-scanner; DAST: OWASP ZAP nightly; Secrets: gitleaks pre-commit; IaC: tfsec on plan." ❌ Tooling is "TBD" at GA; no automated gates. |
| SS-026 | Pen-test legal authorisation in place | §4.5 Security Testing `[13-5]` | Medium | Any active scanning / pen testing is performed within legal boundaries with proper authorisation, scope, time windows, and data-handling rules. | ✅ Signed rules of engagement; pre-test notice; debrief and remediation backlog. ❌ Tester runs sqlmap against prod without notice; potential legal exposure. |
| SS-027 | Compiler / build warnings treated as errors | CERT #2 Heed compiler warnings `[13-5]` | Low | Build pipeline treats compiler/linker warnings (and equivalent language linters) as errors; deviations are explicit and reviewed. | ✅ `-Werror` enabled; lint configured as required check. ❌ Thousands of warnings ignored; a real defect hides among them. |
| SS-028 | Adoption of a documented coding standard (security-aware) | CERT #10 Adopt a software construction security standard `[13-5]` | Low | The team adopts a security-aware coding standard (CERT C/C++ Coding Standard, OWASP ASVS, MISRA, ISO/IEC 25024) and references it in the SRS / constitution. | ✅ "Java code follows OWASP ASVS L2; CERT Oracle Coding Standard for Java enforced via SpotBugs Security plugin." ❌ No standard adopted; each developer invents their own. |
